macro_segments.title: Introduction and Overview of the Debate Between Rabbi Meir, Rabbi Shimon, and the Chachamim

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and framing of the daf's central debate
The three positions: Rabbi Meir (roofs and courtyards are each one domain), Rabbi Shimon (all combined are one domain), and the Chachamim (each is distinct)
Clarification of the practical difference: what vessels can move between domains without an eruv
Detailed example of yards A and B: what happens with eruv vs. without eruv

macro_segments.title: The Baraita Supporting Rav's Position on Roofs and the Position of Rabbi Meir

micro_segments.title: Baraita: 'All roofs of the city are one domain' (Rabbi Meir's position), but one cannot go from roof to courtyard or vice versa
The Chachamim's position in the baraita: each roof is a separate domain; on one roof you can only move four amot

macro_segments.title: Baraita Supporting Rabbi Yehuda's Explanation: The Oil and Towels Story at Rabbi Shimon's Academy

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda: 'We taught like Rabbi Shimon'; story of bathing at Rabbi Shimon's yeshiva in Tekoa—moving oil from roof to courtyard to karpef
Alternative story: moving a Torah scroll from courtyard to roof to karpef in time of danger; the Chachamim's objection that emergencies are not proof

macro_segments.title: Rav's Ruling and the Debate Between Rav and Shmuel over Whether an Eruv Allows Carrying Between Yards

micro_segments.title: Rav: 'The halakha is like Rabbi Shimon'—but only if they did not make an eruv (vehu shelo eirvu)
Rav's concern: if A made an eruv, you can carry house vessels from A's houses into A's yard, so now there will be confusion about which vessels started in the yard and which came from the house
Shmuel's position: 'Whether they made an eruv or not'—we are not afraid of confusion; you can still carry yard vessels from A to B even if A made an eruv
Rabbi Yochanan agrees with Shmuel; the challenging question from Rav Chisda: how will you distinguish which vessel you can carry where if there are two vessels in the same yard?

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Position According to His Own Logic: No Gezeirah Against Confusion

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon does not make a gezeirah (decree) out of fear of confusion; proof from the case of three courtyards open to each other
The three courtyards case: A and C each made an eruv with B, but not with each other; A can go to B and B to C, but not A to C—yet Rabbi Shimon allows yard vessels to travel between them
The conclusion: Rabbi Shimon's position is that even with an eruv, yard vessels can move between similar domains because he is not concerned about vessel confusion

macro_segments.title: The Baraita on Balcony and Courtyard: Proof That the Chachamim Hold a Middle Position

micro_segments.title: Case: balcony and courtyard that did not make an eruv; determining ownership by height (higher than 10 tefachim belongs to balcony, less belongs to courtyard)
The rule applies when many people live on each level and each group made their own eruv; the Chachamim's position: if no eruv was made at all, all domains become one
The baraita lists: roof, courtyard, portico (achsadra), and balcony (mirpeset)—but notably omits karpef and mavoi, suggesting the Chachamim distinguish less-used spaces

macro_segments.title: The Baraita on Balcony and Courtyard: Proof That the Chachamim Hold a Middle Position (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's inference: this is not Rabbi Shimon's position (he would include everything); this is the Chachamim, who see a middle ground between Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Shimon

macro_segments.title: The Case of Five Courtyards Opening into an Alley: Rabbi Shimon vs. the Chachamim on Domain Boundaries

micro_segments.title: Scenario: five courtyards open to each other and all open to a mavoi (alley); nobody made an eruv at all
The Chachamim's position (presumed from the baraita): you cannot carry from courtyard to mavoi or from mavoi to courtyard—they are distinct domains
But courtyard vessels within a courtyard can be carried; however, mavoi vessels within a mavoi are restricted (only 4 amot)
Rabbi Shimon's addition to the baraita: 'As long as there are many people and nobody made an eruv, roof, courtyard, portico, balcony, karpef, and mavoi are all one domain'

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Interpretation of Rabbi Shimon's Ruling: The 'No Eruv' Phrase and Concession to Opponents

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's initial question: why does Rabbi Shimon say 'only if they did not make an eruv'? Does he mean to restrict his ruling to that case?
Answer: 'No eruv' means they did not make a joint eruv uniting all the courtyards and the mavoi; it does not mean there is no eruv within courtyard A

Alternative interpretation: Rabbi Shimon speaks to those who oppose him, conceding that if there were an eruv in A causing confusion, you might have a problem; but he himself would say there is no confusion to fear
The Chachamim's counter: we are not afraid of confusion; the point is that mavoi is a different genre of domain altogether—separate domains by nature, not by confusion concerns

macro_segments.title: Final Resolution: The Status of Mavoi Vessels and Why Mavoi Differs from Courtyard

micro_segments.title: Question: if a vessel starts in a mavoi (not in a courtyard), can you carry it within the mavoi itself without a shituf (contribution agreement)?
Citation of Rabbi Zeira in the name of Rav: a mavoi without a shituf allows carrying only 4 amot, unlike courtyard vessels
Possible explanation: a mavoi has some qualities of a carmelit (semi-public domain), hence the stricter limit; alternative explanations involve confusion of vessel types

Final summary: the Chachamim hold that mavoi and karpef are separate from the regularly-used domains (roof, courtyard, portico, balcony); Rabbi Shimon includes everything; Rabbi Meir makes each one distinct

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Shimon (positions on domain unity)
Rav (early Amora, rules like Rabbi Shimon)
Shmuel (early Amora, disagrees with Rav on eruv confusion)
Rav Chisda (raises objection about vessel confusion)
carmelit / karmellt (semi-public domain with special rules)
vessel confusion / house vessels vs. yard vessels (key legal concern)
bathing and oil (practical Shabbat example from Tekoa)
different ownership (basis for Rabbi Meir's distinction of roofs)
